hints: Condition on the one special coin — the fair one.
Even total = (fair H and odd among the rest) or (fair T and even among the rest).
If p = P(even among the n−1), the answer is ½p + ½(1−p) = ½, regardless of λ.
Think about how the game can possibly end.
It ends on a round where the surviving coins are all-tails (lose) or all-heads (win).
Those two terminal patterns are equally likely on that final round, so P(win)=½.
Use the symmetry X ↦ 1−X of the uniform distribution.
Replacing each X by 1−X turns min into 1−max and max into 1−min.
That maps {U+V>1} to {U+V<1} with equal probability, so each is ½.
Find the 'checkmate' totals you want to land on, working back from 50.
If you say 39 you can always complement to 50 (you+opponent add 11 per round).
Back up by 11s: 50,39,28,17,6. Say 6 first, then mirror to keep adding 11.
Write the payout as an expectation over N, the flip of the first head.
N ~ Geom(½), payout 2^N, so E = Σ 2^n·(1/2)^n.
Each term equals 1, so the sum diverges → fair value is infinite (enter −1).
Roll again only while the expected change from one more roll is positive.
One more roll: 1/10 chance lose everything (current total k), 9/10 chance gain avg 5.
Stop when expected gain 0: solve E = (9/10)(5+E) ⇒ E=45, so k=45.
T,G,P are IID Geom(½); write the joint PMF and the ordered region T<G<P.
Sum innermost over p>g, then g>t (geometric tails collapse nicely).
Impose the martingale condition E[M_{n+1} | F_n] = M_n.
M_{n+1}=M_n·e^{X_{n+1}+c}, and X is ±1 equally likely, so E[e^{X+c}]=e^c·(e+e^{-1})/2.
Set that to 1: c = −log(½e^1 + ½e^{-1}), so a=½,b=1,c=½,d=−1 and a+b+c+d=1.
solution:
Apply the martingale condition $\mathbb{E}[M_{n+1}\mid\mathcal{F}_n]=M_n$. Since $M_{n+1}=M_n e^{X_{n+1}+c}$ and $M_n$ is $\mathcal{F}_n$-measurable,
$$\mathbb{E}[M_{n+1}\mid\mathcal{F}_n]=M_n\,e^{c}\,\mathbb{E}[e^{X_{n+1}}]=M_n\,e^{c}\cdot\tfrac{1}{2}\big(e^{1}+e^{-1}\big).$$
For this to equal $M_n$ we need $e^{c}\cdot\tfrac12(e+e^{-1})=1$, i.e.
$$c=-\log\!\Big(\tfrac12 e^{1}+\tfrac12 e^{-1}\Big).$$
Matching the form $-\log(ae^{b}+ce^{d})$ gives $a=\tfrac12,\,b=1,\,c=\tfrac12,\,d=-1$, so $a+b+c+d=1$.

hints: Rearrange xy = x+y into x = y/(y−1).
You need both x and y written with only the digit 1 — try a repunit for y.
Take y=11 ⇒ x=11/10=1.1 (all ones). Then xy = 1.1×11 = 12.1 (and indeed x+y=12.1).
solution: From $xy=x+y$ we get $x(y-1)=y$, so $x=\dfrac{y}{y-1}$. We need both numbers to use only the digit $1$. Try the repunit $y=11$: then $x=\dfrac{11}{10}=1.1$, which uses only $1$s. Check: $x+y=1.1+11=12.1$ and $xy=1.1\times 11=12.1$. Both conditions hold, so $xy=12.1$.

hints: Let a be the 40-die value and b the 60-die value; you want P(b>a).
Condition on a (uniform on 1..40): P(b>a)=(60−a)/60.
solution:
Let $a$ be the value of the $40$-sided die and $b$ the value of the $60$-sided die, independent and uniform. Condition on $a$: given $a$, the $60$-die exceeds it with probability $\dfrac{60-a}{60}$ (values $a+1,\dots,60$). Averaging over $a\in\{1,\dots,40\}$,
$$\mathbb{P}[b>a]=\frac{1}{40}\sum_{a=1}^{40}\frac{60-a}{60}=\frac{1}{2400}\sum_{a=1}^{40}(60-a).$$
Now $\sum_{a=1}^{40}(60-a)=\sum_{k=20}^{59}k=\dfrac{(20+59)\cdot 40}{2}=1580$, giving $\dfrac{1580}{2400}=\dfrac{79}{120}.$
